text: The surgeon performs a peritumoral injection into the subsorosal layer, with indocyanine green (ICG) visibly passing to the drain and leaf nodes within fifteen minutes.
The surgeon visualizes the fluorescence emitted by indocyanine green (ICG) under near-infrared light, highlighting lymphatic structures during the robotic colonic resection.
The surgeon accurately identifies the lymphatic drainage patterns of the tumor, visually confirming left colic drainage using fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon employs an infracolic three-step approach to mobilize the splenic flexure using a single jockeying technique, manipulating the colon and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses special camera filters to visualize the ICG fluorescence, switching between normal and fluorescence viewing modes by pressing a pedal, all in high definition.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using robotic hemologs, ensuring precise control and visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas and carefully separates its tail from the splenic flexure of the colon, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon divides the mesentery up to the level of the small bowel, preparing for colonic transection.
The surgeon administers another volume of indocyanine green (ICG) to visualize the blood supply at both ends of the bowel near the transection site, using fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform an intracorporeal anastomosis, connecting the transected ends of the bowel within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a continuous suture to close the enterotomy, carefully stitching the edges of the bowel incision together.
The surgeon performs a second layer of sutures in a continuous running fashion to reinforce and cover the anastomosis site.
